name: Polypodium leucotomos
oneliner: Most-studied oral photoprotectant. Adjunct, not replacement.
headline: Polypodium leucotomos

evidence.outcome: Significant ↑ MED (minimum erythema dose) at 60 d

forms.name: Ultra Capsules
forms.why: The clinical-trial brand. Most studies use this exact extract.

forms.name: Daily Capsules
forms.why: Same patented extract under a different label. Same evidence.

forms.name: PLE 250 mg
forms.why: Generic PLE. Cheaper, no third-party confirmation of active fraction.

faq.a: No, and no credible practitioner has ever claimed it does. Polypodium adds a small fraction of additional protection from the inside — typically described as 'one-fifth of an SPF.' It's an adjunct, not a substitute. Skip the sunscreen and you skip the protection.

faq.q: Will I notice anything in a week?
faq.a: Unlikely. The clinical signal builds at 6–8 weeks. Expect to commit to a full 12-week trial before deciding whether it's working for you.

The longest published safety dataset is around 12 months at 480 mg/day. For longer use, build in a 4-week pause every 6 months and discuss with your physician — particularly if you take any anticoagulants.

faq.q: Why is it Tier B and not Tier A?
faq.a: Because the clinical trials are small (typically n < 50), the effect sizes are modest, and the long-term outcomes are not as robust as we'd want for a Tier A. The molecule is supported, but the data isn't yet conclusive.
